# Transcrição: Train My Traffic Person Session 7

- **Fonte:** Train My Traffic Person Session 7
- **Duração:** 1:15:08
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

When you launch a campaign, you should have one success metric that judges whether this campaign is a failure or a success. Really just one. It doesn't mean that there aren't going to be other metrics that you look at to try to figure out how certain parts of the campaign are performing. But you should really have one success metric. And we talked a lot about this in module one when we went over the traffic system. You guys can see on your traffic system sheets that for every step of the traffic system, every campaign that you're going to build, there is a section for your success metric. Usually this success metric is cost per acquisition, return on ad spend. Sometimes it's cost per lead, cost per webinar sign up. But you really need to know how am I judging the success of this campaign that I'm setting up? And if you don't know that, then after you launch the campaign and you've let it run a few days and you have data, you don't have a value system to use or you don't have a rubric to use to help you judge whether that campaign succeeded or not. So as I said, for most of you guys, it will be something like return on ad spend, cost per acquisition, cost per lead. And make sure that you always have that established before you launch a campaign because that is the one metric that you're going to use to say, did this work? And should I scale it? Or did this fail? And do I need to move more into optimization and troubleshooting to figure out why it didn't fail? Because if the campaign succeeds out of the gate, great, it's time for scale. And if not, and for me, this is still about 60% of the time. When I first got started, I would say it was more like 90-10. So 90% of the time after I launched a campaign, right out of the gate, the first three to five days, it would fail. I'd say in air quotes here in regards to my success metric, and 10% of the time it would win. Now, that doesn't mean that the next iteration of the campaign didn't succeed or the next iteration. But sometimes you've got to come back four, five, six, seven times just continuing to make changes to really figure out what works. Nowadays, because I have more experience, it's more like 60% of the time right out of the gate. It looks like a failure. And 40% of the time it's more of a winner. And this really comes from experience. And as I've said to you guys multiple times over the last few months, really understanding that this is a skill set that you're developing, that I want you to be able to apply to any situation because as we'll talk about later, this topic of troubleshooting, hey, Molly, my campaign didn't work. What do I do next? There are lots of layers to that.

## [03:01 - 06:04]

This is a very reactive conversation with hundreds, I would say even thousands of variables that are really going into play here when we look at the success of a campaign. And the more that we do this, the more that we become familiar because of the experiences that we've had in identifying why something is working or why something isn't working. So don't be discouraged if your campaigns aren't working right out of the gate because me, eight years or so into this already, I'm still failing right out of the gate 60% of the time. And that's totally fine. That's not what dictates us being a good media buyer or a bad media buyer. It's that ability to really dive in and figure out why it didn't work so that we can fix it and our ability to keep getting back up and keep trying new things. So just to set some expectations here for my success rate, and I'm sure those of you who are experienced have seen this over your career as a media buyer. The more experience you have and the more that you push forward, you'll start to see that success rate increase over time. But when a campaign is underperforming, most media buyers don't know why. So this is a huge mistake or issue that I see with media buyers. As I was saying earlier, a campaign doesn't work and we just blame Facebook or we give up or we just don't really know what to do. It's like, okay, this isn't working, but I don't know what to do next. And one of the skills of a really good media buyer is the ability to be the detective as we talked about in the first module and the ability to figure out, okay, this didn't work, but what can I learn from this campaign and why isn't it working and what do I need to do to fix it? Optimization is really just about discovering what's wrong with your campaign and creating a hypothesis on how to fix it. That's what I'm going to teach you today. And hopefully if you have a campaign that's not working or hasn't worked in the past, you can use this framework that I'm about to share to go in and say, oh, I actually think that everything about my campaign is pretty good, but the ad copy was just really not resonating with the audience. So I'm going to set up a new campaign with new ad copy or Molly. I think it was actually my landing page that wasn't converting and I'm going to run some new tasks and create some fixes on my page so that we can get that conversion rate up and so that this campaign can be a success. That's the type of conversation that I want to hear after this presentation, okay, a campaign didn't work, but what didn't work about it? And maybe it's Molly, nothing worked. That happened. It was a complete failure and that's okay.

## [06:04 - 09:07]

That just means we need to go back to the drawing board and start from scratch. So this is really about discovering what's wrong with the campaign, pinpointing what we think is wrong, coming up with a hypothesis and a plan and really a way to fix what we think failed about our campaign. As I was saying earlier, guys, this is not black and white. This is not a simple exercise. We've got to put our detective hats on and we also have to realize that, as I said earlier, there are hundreds and maybe even thousands of variables at play here. This is not necessarily easy. This is hard work, but we must do it and this is such a huge and important part of being a media buyer. Now I've shared this a few times throughout the training, but just a little bit of an easy button. If you run into situations where campaigns just aren't working or they might seem buggy or things just don't seem to be necessarily working the way that you wanted, a great easy button to restart the optimization process with Facebook is to duplicate and restart a campaign. So as we talked about, or I'm sorry, as we'll talk about here in a minute, one issue that I run into sometimes and that you guys will too, is that you launch a campaign a few days go by and nothing happens. No reach, no sales, nothing. Sometimes that can be a Facebook bug, sometimes there can be other issues, but in those cases, sometimes all you need to do is simply duplicate the campaign and restart. This obviously isn't going to fix a bad offer, bad targeting, bad copy, bad creative, all the things that we know are so important based off of the last two months that we've spent together. But what this does, this easy button is it actually restarts the optimization process. So everything that we talked about in module three, how Facebook's auction works, all of that really comes back into play here in a big way because when you start a new campaign or you duplicate and you restart a campaign, you are restarting that optimization process. You are re-entering the auction. That's why right now in Q4, I could take maybe a Facebook campaign that's been running for three or four months, that's been doing really well, duplicate it, restart it, exact same thing that I've been running three or four months and you'll see that the cost per acquisition is probably 30 to 40% more than it has been in that campaign over the last three months. The reason for that is that it's the holidays and so ad costs are more expensive. And because you're duplicating and restarting now towards the end of November versus late

## [09:07 - 12:09]

summer, you are entering the auction in a very different time and place than you did three or four months ago. So sometimes this plays to our disadvantage as I was just saying, but sometimes this plays to our advantage. Duplicating and restarting and really re-entering that auction and restarting this optimization process with Facebook. So the easy button when in doubt, a duplicate and restart can go a long way. And as I said, it can't fix core marketing issues like your offer or your targeting or your copy or creative, but sometimes it can help if you get in a sticky situation. This is the exact, these are the exact steps that I would go through. Now some of them are going to open up rabbit holes because some of them are going to check out fine. You're going to look at step one and you're going to say, no, Molly, I'm not having those problems. Okay, great. Move to the second step. But sometimes when you go through this process, you're going to identify something like, oh, wow, Molly, like it's really my landing page that isn't converting. And that might lead you down a rabbit hole. Let yourself do that, right? As you start to uncover possible issues with your campaign, research that as much as you feel called to do so, or you might get to that step and say, no, I'm doing good in this category check mark. I'm going to move forward. So this is going to be different for everybody because you guys are all having different experiences and your campaigns are performing in different ways. And every one of those steps that I just mentioned, there are optimization opportunities there and every step should be looked at individually when it comes to the optimization and troubleshooting

process and I always work backwards. What I mean from that is I always start with the page essentially. So I'm working backwards in the business's mind if that makes sense to you guys. ## [21:18 - 24:19]

But policy can be a huge reason that you're not getting results. Something else that affects a lot of clients too is billing. So making sure that the credit card that you're using is always active and able to process payments. This can really obviously affect your reach because Facebook will stop running your campaigns. Something else to think about is objective in combination with audience size and budget. So we talked a lot about this, but if you are targeting maybe 50 or 100 people, you might find that you simply aren't getting any reach. That is just too small of an audience size for Facebook to work with. Or for example, if you're using the conversion objective to an audience size less than 5,000 people, that could be really hard for Facebook to digest. We talked about all of this during week three. But this can be a symptom of making poor decisions when it comes to objective audience size and budget. A big symptom of that can be, wow, I'm just not getting any reach or I'm just getting a little bit of reach and just not much is happening inside of this campaign. So if you're struggling with this, definitely come to me. I can help you identify maybe which of these is causing that. And for sure, go back to module three. But if you have an issue with reach, definitely check policy stuff, billing, and most of the time it comes down to using the wrong objective, audience size, or budget for what you're actually trying to accomplish with your campaign. ## [24:19 - 27:23]

But for most of this, this means, you know, are they doing what I want them to do on my website? And is it happening in the way that I expected? And when I'm looking at this aspect of the campaign, I'm definitely analyzing first off conversion rate on the page. So again, a lot of the optimization that needs to be done here with our campaign guys actually has nothing to do with Facebook. Lots of different companies that allow you to do heat maps and basically heat maps record people's activity on your site so that you can see, you know, if someone lands on my site, are they just immediately abandoning? So is there an ad scent or congruency issue here where maybe they were interested in my ad, but once they clicked over to the page, there just wasn't congruency or something turned them off and they hit the back button. ## [27:23 - 30:26]

map training that John did for TMTP Lasco round because nothing has changed with that because a video below this training here in this module so that you guys can go ahead and go through that installing your heat maps and just figuring out, you know, John talks about how to read the heat maps, how to really what that onsite optimization looks like. But when it comes to post click experience, there are really two things I'm starting to look at. The first is the conversion rate. If the conversion rate looks good, then I move on and I say, Hey, my post click is converting. Well, it must have to do something, you know, I have to do with something, you know, with the actual ads, let's move on. But if the conversion rate doesn't look so hot, that is when I move to the heat map portion and John's training will show you guys how to analyze that, which is really exciting. And I just want to give you some benchmark conversion rates if you are newer to this and don't know what good or bad is. Here are a few different types of pages that you guys are running traffic to. The first is a lead magnet landing page where you're sending traffic from an ad to a page where you ask someone to fill out form fields, give you information in exchange for something of value. So this is a webinar, a free video series, a lead magnet, maybe even a coupon. I call this a landing, a lead magnet landing page. Some people call this a squeeze page. But anything under 30%, when you're giving something away for free, anything under a 30% conversion rate on a page like this is pretty low. And it means that you either need to work on the page and figure out with the heat maps, is there something on my page that's broken, or you need to consider is this offer, this lead magnet, whatever I'm giving away for free, is it just not sexy enough for people to opt into? But if it's lower than 30%, it definitely needs some work. I mean, really solid lead magnet pages convert 60, 70, 80, sometimes 90%. And that's when you see lead cost of 50 cents of a dollar because that conversion rate is so high. So this is a benchmark here for you guys sending traffic to those sorts of pages. If you're sending more to a tripwire sales page or a pre-sell article where maybe you're doing a free plus shipping offer or you've got a pre-sell article that's then making a call to action for a product, I usually expect these types of pages to convert it around 10%. So for example, the free sticker offer that you guys saw with the apparel brand that I work with, and I'll go more into those campaigns today. Anything below a 10% makes me think, okay, maybe the offer needs some work here, maybe

## [30:26 - 33:31]

there's something going on with the page. Anything above 10% is really, really good. For those of you guys with campaigns that are going direct to a product page, store, catalog, you're going to see a much lower conversion rate because you're pretty much going directly to a full price product or multiple full price products and asking people to buy. Anything over 3% here is really good. And yeah, honestly, 3%, even 2% I'm pretty happy with. If you have any other types of pages that I didn't cover in these three types of pages, just ask me what you think the conversion rate should be. What we decided to test was a version of that page that basically just chopped the whole bottom half part off, made it much more simple and shorter.

## [39:35 - 42:38]

go to the ads. Are my ads doing what they need to do, or isn't my ads that are really underperforming here? After we get past the am I getting reach stage? Guys, it's usually one of these two things. It's usually the post-click experience or the ads, honestly. The next thing that I look at is, are my ads working? I start to analyze a few things. The first is the unique click-through rate. This is a good indicator of, are people interested in my ads? If it's above 1%, that's pretty good. If it's above 2% to 3%, that's really, really good. If it's below 1%, and that's not great. So let's test something with a new destination. So maybe trying this exact campaign, that's going to be my next step. So what do you do? I figured out maybe why or the multiple reasons why my ads aren't working, what should I do? And sometimes it's the campaign needs more time. Sometimes you need to make some adjustments to the campaign that's live, but that is much rarer than most of you think. And then option three is sometimes it means you just need to set up a brand new test campaign based off of your findings. Those are really your three options. Keep running the campaign, collect more data, make some small tweaks to your current campaign give it another three days, see what happens or build a completely new test campaign based off of your findings and what you know you need to fix. A big mistake that a lot of you guys are making is making too many tweaks and changing things in your campaigns too frequently. That is causing Facebook to restart their optimization process and it's not healthy. So I'm really speaking to that second option here. What do you do if you want to make tweaks to a certain campaign? If your budgets are set at the ad set level, it's okay to turn off ad sets that are under performing that aren't meeting your success metric. It's okay to turn off ads that are under performing. So even going to the ad level and looking at return on ads, been CPA, cost for lead and turning off ads that are underperforming. I really don't recommend this as much but it is okay if you just feel called to do that and that's the type of media buying you want to do. If budgets are at the campaign level and you're using CBO, which by the way I'm doing a training all about CBO next week inside of team traffic that I'm actually I'm also going to add to your old members area, which is really exciting. But if your campaign is built for CBO, then it changes a little bit. You don't want to turn anything off with CBO because of the way the optimization is working.

## [1:10:01 - 1:13:02]

So if you turn an ad set off when you're using CBO, it changes the optimization dynamics of the campaign. Same thing with your ads. If you really want to turn ads off with CBO, it can be okay, but I really don't recommend it. Something I do recommend is it's okay to add new ads to your ad sets when you're using CBO. So for example, if you have a CBO campaign that's just not performing the way that you want and you've diagnosed that it's your ads and for some reason you don't want to start a brand new campaign. You could try just going in and adding one to two new ads to each ad set, right? The same one to two ads to each ad set. But sometimes you will see that that gives the campaign a lift and can refresh it and gives Facebook some new ads to work with. And because you're using CBO and campaigns at the budget level, the addition of those new ads normally doesn't negatively affect the campaign. If you're using budgets at the ad set level, I really don't recommend adding new ads to a campaign that's actively running because it will throw off that optimization process. So number one, you're either going to let the campaigns continue to run and collect data. Number two, you're going to make some tweaks to the campaigns if certain ads or ad sets aren't meeting your expectations. And if your budget's at the ad set level, it's okay to turn off an ad set that's underperforming. If you're using CBO, I really would try not to do that. But I would think about adding one to two ads to each ad set if the ads are the issue that you've identified and see if you have a chance to really freshen things up. But my favorite is number three, take the learnings from your test campaign that you ran and set up a brand new campaign. You know, that's reflecting what you think was wrong with your old campaign. If you think you used the wrong objective, duplicate, change that objective and restart. If you figured out that it was your ads, create a new campaign with some new ads.
